title: Chapter 24: The Third Facility
content: Greenwich Wellness Center looked like a country club.

It was intentional. The founder — a psychiatrist named Dr. Eleanor Vance who'd trained at Yale and consulted for McKinsey before deciding that institutionalizing inconvenient daughters was more profitable than advising corporations — had designed the facility to look exactly like the places where the families who sent their daughters spent their weekends. Same architecture. Same landscaping. Same color palette of cream and sage and "calming" earth tones. The message was subliminal but clear: *this isn't a prison. This is just another exclusive institution your family belongs to.*
The patients, twenty-seven of them, were less convinced.

Anna Delgado had been on the ground in Connecticut for three days, posing as a prospective family member — the same cover she'd used at Serenity, updated with a new backstory about a "troubled niece" who needed "structure." She'd toured the facility twice. She'd catalogued the same patterns: cameras in every room, medication rounds three times daily, group therapy sessions where "progress" was measured by how enthusiastically patients agreed with their diagnoses.
She'd also noticed something different. The staff was scared.

"They've seen what happened to Serenity and Aurora," she reported, video-calling from her hotel room in Stamford. "The orderlies are jumpy. The nurses are double-checking medication logs. The acting director — Vance died last year, same pattern as Webb — has been in meetings with lawyers for three days straight. They know we're coming. They just don't know when."
"Good," Chloe said. "Fear makes them sloppy. Sloppy makes them vulnerable."
"Do we have inside contacts?"

"Three." Layla pulled up the messages on her tablet. "Keisha, twenty-four, committed by her grandmother for 'religious deviance' — she converted to Buddhism. Priya, twenty-two, committed by her parents for refusing to drop out of medical school to get married. Jasmine, nineteen, committed by her father for reporting his business partner's sexual harassment to HR."
"Same pattern," Rachel said. "Every facility. Every state. Women committed for refusing to comply with what their families wanted them to be."

"Same vulnerabilities too." Layla swiped to a network diagram. "Greenwich uses the same cloud provider as Serenity and Aurora. Different facility, same default passwords. The IT contractor is a local firm that services three other facilities in the tri-state area. Same firm, same configurations, same vulnerabilities. I can get into their network from here."
"And the inside assets?"

"Keisha has a phone. Same situation as Jamie — orderly forgot to confiscate it. She's been documenting conditions for four months. Jasmine works in the facility library — it has a computer with internet access, unmonitored because no one thinks patients use the library. Priya's roommate was discharged last week and left her a smartwatch that connects to the guest Wi-Fi."
"Three devices. Three women. Three access points to the outside." Chloe looked at the map. "What's the timeline?"

"I can have the network mapped by tonight," Layla said. "Camera loop script is already adapted from Aurora — same camera model, same firmware. I need forty-eight hours to prepare the file extraction."
"I can be back inside the facility by Wednesday," Anna said. "Second tour. 'Follow-up questions.' I'll bring a hidden camera this time. Vasquez has the equipment."
"The inside assets are ready," Jamie said. "I've been messaging them. They've got five more patients who want to testify. Total of eight. Same number we had at Aurora."

Chloe looked around the room. At every woman who'd done this before. Who'd built the playbook, tested it, refined it. Who'd turned what was supposed to be a one-time prison break into a replicable process.
"Then we go Wednesday," she said. "Same playbook. Network access, file extraction, media publication, patient testimony, facility suspension. By Friday, Greenwich is the third red X on the map."
Wednesday arrived faster than anyone expected.

Layla got into Greenwich's network at 11:00 PM Tuesday. The admin credentials were, as predicted, the cloud provider's factory defaults. The camera system was, as predicted, the same model as Aurora's. The patient files were, as predicted, stored on an unencrypted server with access logs that hadn't been reviewed since the founder died.

At 2:00 AM Wednesday, Anna Delgado walked into Greenwich Wellness Center for her "follow-up tour" — a hidden camera in her blazer button, a backup recorder in her pen, and a list of questions designed to make the acting director incriminate herself on tape.

At 2:30 AM, Keisha, Jasmine, and Priya met in the facility library — the one room with a computer, the one room with no camera — and began downloading patient files while the night orderly made his rounds.

At 3:00 AM, they had everything: twenty-seven intake files, twenty-seven billing records, an internal memo from the acting director to the facility's legal team titled "Containment Strategy — Women of Serenity Investigation" that outlined exactly how the facility planned to discredit patient testimony.
At 6:00 AM, Anna Delgado published the article:
**THE THIRD SERENITY: GREENWICH WELLNESS CENTER**
At 9:00 AM, the Connecticut Department of Public Health suspended the facility's license.
At noon, the acting director resigned.
At 3:00 PM, twenty-seven women were discharged.
Three facilities down. Eight to go.
That night, in the Boulder Airbnb, Chloe updated the map.
She took the red marker — the same one she'd used to cross out Serenity four weeks ago, Aurora two weeks ago — and drew a third X across Connecticut.

Behind her, the room was fuller than it had ever been. Layla and Sophia and Maya and Rachel and Vasquez and Jamie and Nia and Sara and Elena and the three volunteers from Denver and now four new women who'd driven in from Colorado Springs after seeing the Greenwich news coverage. They were running out of chairs. They were running out of floor space. They were running out of everything except momentum.
"Three down," Chloe said.
"Eight to go," Maya finished.
"And the Alliance?"

Layla looked up from her tablet. "They issued a statement. Called it 'further evidence of a coordinated attack on legitimate medical providers.' Same playbook. Same words. They're running out of variations."
"Good," Chloe said. "When your enemy starts repeating themselves, it means they're out of ideas."

She looked at the map — eight red dots still glowing. Eight facilities. Eight targets. And behind her, a room full of women who'd learned, in the past six weeks, that the system was breakable. That the walls were permeable. That the people who'd locked them up had spent so long believing they were invincible that they'd forgotten to check if their doors were actually locked.

"Number four," Chloe said, "is going to be faster than number three. And number five faster than number four. Because every facility we close teaches us something. And every facility we close teaches them — the other facilities, the ones still operating — that they're next."
She turned to face the room.

"The Alliance tried to arrest Layla. They failed. They tried to fund a counter-organization. They're failing. They tried to put Holloway on cable news. Everyone who watched that interview now knows her name, her face, and what she did at Serenity. Every move they make, we turn into a weapon. Every escalation they attempt, we escalate back harder."
She picked up the marker again. Held it out.
"Who wants to cross out number four?"
Jamie took the marker.
